2. Nice python featuers

  • Zip
  • Unpacking

  • List comprehensions

  • Generators

2.1. Iterators and Generators

zip, map, and enumerate return iterators, which are lazy, not subscriptable, and get exhausted after one pass. Generators provide an easy way to create iterators.

If the code for a function contains a yield then the function always returns a generator object.

def gen():
    print('hi')
    if False:
        yield 1
        yield 2
    return 1

type(gen()) # <class 'generator'>

# gen() is a generator not 1

Python sets up a local frame. Calling next with a generator passed in will cause Python to start running the body until we see a yield, pause the execution and give us that value that was yielded. We can also loop over genertaors

Python range does not store a generator. It stores the arithmetic to generate elements lazily. It differs from a generator in that it is a (reusable) sequence and not an iterator You can index via range(a,b,c)[1] for example. Generators usually do not know their length cheaply but range does. It’s best to think of range like a lazy sequence and generator as a stream.

Example: Return all ordered subsets of array

yield from keyword:

Recall that parentheses on their own (no comma) do not make a tuple.
